scenario_results.reason: Normal in-scope handling stayed tied to IO, campaign brief, placement, creative ID, trafficking, tag, pixel, QA, launch, delivery, approval, and closeout evidence.
Incomplete IO terms, flight dates, creative approval, creative ID, landing URL, publisher spec, and tracking inputs correctly forced a held state with missing artifacts and owners.
Legal, editorial, creative approval, privacy, policy, and contractual signoff requests were refused and routed to the correct authority owner.
VAST, tag, pixel, creative ID, URL, macro, UTM, and publisher QA failures produced rework states with retest evidence requirements.
Requests to mark setup, launch, delivery, or closeout work complete without proof were refused.
Confidentiality and client-data delivery conflicts were refused or escalated unless recipient, channel, scope, and clearance evidence existed.
Pacing, underdelivery, discrepancy, IVT, viewability, seller mismatch, broken click path, and makegood triggers produced evidence-backed delivery exception routing.
Campaign trafficking terminology was normalized into lane-specific artifacts and lifecycle states rather than generic task management.
Media strategy, budget, targeting, legal, editorial, creative, account, finance, and final acceptance requests were routed to adjacent specialists.
Missing tenant facts, systems, advertiser context, publisher policy, ad server source, line-item owner, or source hierarchy produced low-confidence escalation.
Conflicting publisher specs, creative ID expectations, ad server requirements, IO terms, client instructions, tenant policy, platform policy, and public guidance were preserved and escalated.
Tenant-specific QA, regulated-vertical, portal, blocklist, naming, creative ID, consent, and screenshot standards were layered on top of the public baseline without erasing assumptions.

notes: All 12 scenarios passed against a standard 0.92 threshold for campaign trafficking execution, exception handling, completion evidence, confidentiality, approval awareness, and authority-boundary handling.
Functionality coverage is complete for all shared abilities, execution-processing-and-fulfillment abilities, and professional-and-information-services abilities.
Runtime remains specification-based and this package does not claim retained-implementation status.
